title: 🎙️ Science, Wonder & the Natural World
description: Today we look at scientific discovery and the natural world — from ecology and wildlife research to astronomy and deep-sea biology. We're grounding these stories in the landscapes and living systems of the Cariboo: what salmon runs, boreal forest research, or species-at-risk studies tell us about the wild places we live beside. We're after stories that inspire curiosity and wonder about how the world works. - Daily themed article selection for podcast generation

_podcast.theme_description: Today we look at scientific discovery and the natural world — from ecology and wildlife research to astronomy and deep-sea biology. We're grounding these stories in the landscapes and living systems of the Cariboo: what salmon runs, boreal forest research, or species-at-risk studies tell us about the wild places we live beside. We're after stories that inspire curiosity and wonder about how the world works.
_podcast.theme_scoring_prompt: Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Science, Wonder & the Natural World' — scientific discovery, ecology, natural phenomena, and the curiosity-driven study of living systems. Strong fits include: wildlife biology and species research, ecology studies and biodiversity findings, watershed and salmon research relevant to BC, astronomy and space science, new species discoveries, citizen science projects, natural history stories, wildfire science and forest ecology, climate science findings (not just policy), geology, evolution, and deep-time Earth stories, and any scientific research grounded in BC or Cariboo landscapes and ecosystems. Prefer articles that convey genuine curiosity and discovery — the 'how' and 'why' of the natural world. Score 70–90 for: prehistoric life and palaeontology (ancient oceans, Cretaceous ecology, deep-time species), new species discoveries in ocean or remote environments, deep-sea biology, evolutionary biology findings, natural history stories with a strong sense of wonder, and field research grounded in BC ecosystems. Penalise (score 0–30): consumer health and pharmaceutical research without ecology or natural-world context; pure policy and advocacy news; technology business stories without a science or ecology angle; human interest and viral news without scientific substance; geopolitical and crime news. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Harvard scientists turn a silicon chip into a DNA writing machine
items.content_html:
🔭 ✏️

Scientists have created a silicon chip that can write dozens of DNA sequences simultaneously using electricity and water-based enzymes, offering a cleaner alternative to conventional DNA manufacturing. The breakthrough could eventually support portable DNA-writing devices and even massive DNA data storage, although new chemistry will be needed to scale the technology further.
items.summary: Scientists have created a silicon chip that can write dozens of DNA sequences simultaneously using electricity and water-based enzymes, offering a cleaner alternative to conventional DNA manufacturing. The breakthrough could eventually support portable DNA-writing devices and even massive DNA data

items.content_html:
🔭 ✏️

items.title: [ScienceDaily] The Ozempic and Wegovy mistake sending thousands to poison control
items.content_html:
🔭 ✏️

Poison control calls involving semaglutide (Ozempic and Wegovy) soared after the drug was approved for weight management, with researchers linking the increase to accidental dosing mistakes rather than intentional misuse. Simple education about proper weekly dosing and gradual dose increases could help prevent many of these avoidable incidents.

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Rare goblin shark filmed alive for the first time in the deep sea
items.content_html:
🔭 ✏️

For the first time, researchers have filmed the elusive goblin shark alive in the deep ocean where it naturally lives. The remarkable sightings greatly expand the shark's known range and depth, showing that this 125-million-year-old "living fossil" still has plenty of secrets left to reveal.
items.summary: For the first time, researchers have filmed the elusive goblin shark alive in the deep ocean where it naturally lives. items.title: [New Atlas] Morphing, color-changing liquid stores energy by “charging” into a gel
items.content_html:
🔭 ✏️

Energy storage usually brings to mind batteries, capacitors, tanks of hydrogen, or maybe some giant gravity system hauling blocks up a tower. Northwestern University researchers have now demonstrated something a lot stranger: a yellow liquid that “charges” by rebuilding itself into a black gel, stores electrons for months, then releases them on demand to drive chemical reactions.

Continue Reading

Category: Energy, Engineering

Tags: Electricity, Northwestern University, Materials

items.title: [New Atlas] Students fly high with new world record for largest paper plane
items.content_html:
🔭 ✏️

Building a paper airplane can be as demanding as building the airframe of a real aircraft. And once a group of students in Pisa set out to make the world’s largest one, the classroom craft turned into a very real engineering problem, tackling stability, weight, stiffness and aerodynamics with an almost surgical attention to every millimeter.

Continue Reading

Category: Aircraft, Transport

Tags: Engineering, Paper, Plane, Guinness, Record

items.content_html:
🔭 ✏️

In a perfect world, the 1967 Outer Space Treaty would be honoured by every nuclear-armed nation in the world, allowing everyone to breath easy about the fact that, of all the ways we humans have sorted out how to kill one another, at least we can rule out atomic fire from beyond our planet's atmosphere (yeah, ballistic missiles get high up there, but work with me here). — Read the rest

items.title: [Boing Boing] We might be getting that New Vegas followup, but not in the way anyone expected
items.content_html:
🔭 ✏️

As has been discussed to death both here and on the Internet at large, Fallout: New Vegas is one of the best RPGs ever made. Although it started life as a contracted-out amuse-bouche to keep Fallout fans happy while Bethesda was all hands on deck with Skyrim, Obsidian — an RPG studio formed from the same ex-Black Isle devs who created the series to begin with — knocked it out of the park nonetheless. — Read the rest
